The good news is that you don’t have to go through what I went through. You can do it in a way that’s healthier for you. Remembering this advice can feel difficult in the moment, but it will push you through to the finish line.

Attack ambiguity

Join a community of people also studying for programming interviews or find a study buddy. Not 100% confident in what you are studying? Flag it. Talk to someone. Don't let ambiguity creep in. A sign that you are a little lost is if you are constantly bouncing between resources and not finishing any of them. Make a plan and stick to it.

Be kind to yourself

It's easy to start putting yourself down, and thinking you don't have what it takes. Failing a leetcode and asking yourself "How was I supposed to know that trick?!" every day will eventually get you feeling like you just don't belong. You do belong. Be patient and recognize you are a bad ass for showing up every day. This shit is hard and takes time to marinate. You are cramming a CS degree into a few months. Don't rush it too much. Even if your interview is in two weeks, you can usually push back to buy yourself more time if needed.

Stay focused

There are a million things that can pull your attention. Building random projects, learning a new language, deep diving into Web3, etc. Time spent on other things is not time spent on moving toward your goal. You can always mix up your study style if you are getting frustrated with the current approach (Ex. Switch to more discussions and mocks if Leetcode is driving you nuts).

Consistency

Especially during the dips in motivation, consistency is key. If you are feeling like crap one day, do your dailies then log off. Don't push it too hard, because it will catch up with you. Your natural motivation will come back, you just need to weather the dip and not be too hard on yourself during that period.

Conclusion

This process isn’t easy for anyone. If it was, everyone would be a software engineer working at Google. Remember that something being hard, just means it takes more time.

Stay focused, attack ambiguity, and most importantly be kind to yourself.

Join the Community

Looking for that study buddy or community of people to hold you accountable? Hackpack is a hyperactive community of engineers all studying for programming interviews.

Apply Here: https://www.hackpack.io/

Big Tech Interview Tips Newsletter
item.author: dannyhabibs

item.title: 在 React 应用中实现 JSON Web Tokens & Passport.js | Implementing JSON Web Tokens & Passport.js in a React App
item.description:
介绍

在你说之前，我知道，我知道。 已经有大量的 MERN 教程展示了如何将 JSON Web Tokens（JWT，发音为“jot”）和 Passport.js 与 Express 一起使用。

但这是每个教程都没有提到的内容，以及我将介绍的内容：如何以及为什么使用 Passport 提供的各种身份验证风格（包括passport-jwt ），以及让我连续数小时绊倒的陷阱一起一个用户注册应用程序。

我不知道你的经历是什么，但我专业从事的大多数项目都已获得其他人预先构建的授权。 因此，除了提高我的 MERN 技能之外，我还认为这是一个了解更多关于安全和身份验证以及不同方法的机会。

这篇文章将主要介绍如何在服务器端实现 Passport 和 JWT，因为这是最神奇的地方，但如果你愿意，你可以在这里查看源文件并下载完整的 MERN 项目。

一旦它正确连接到后端，前端就是纯粹的 React。

不过，在我开始讨论之前，让我简要介绍一下 JWT 和 Passport.js 身份验证。

什么是 JSON Web 令牌？

根据该站点， JSON Web Token是：

从本质上讲，JWT 是可以验证和信任的数字签名令牌，它们在各方（如服务器和客户端）之间的安全和授权以及交换敏感信息方面变得越来越流行，同时验证令牌没有被篡改带或解码。

我不会详细介绍令牌的工作原理，您可以从比我这里更合格的来源阅读所有相关信息，但我只想说，我选择使用 JWT 作为我的用户验证策略的一部分使用 Passport.js 使我的简单用户注册应用程序安全（并且因为我很想知道我是否可以做到，因为我没有很多实践授权的经验）。

有了这些，我的下一个身份验证解决方案是 Passport.js。

什么是 Passport.js 以及我为什么要使用它？

它与 Express.js 配合得非常好。 如果您在 Google 搜索栏中键入术语“JavaScript 身份验证中间件”，甚至只是“JavaScript 身份验证”，Passport.js 将在前 5 个搜索结果中排名。 这就是这个解决方案在 JavaScript 生态系统中的普遍存在。

我有没有提到它拥有 500 多种身份验证策略？ 确实如此。 无论您希望使用简单的用户名和密码登录，还是使用 Github 凭据、Facebook、oAuth 等，都可能有 Passport.js 策略。

所以选择 Passport 作为我授权策略的一部分是一个简单的决定。

我想在网站上添加文档非常好，尽管像自定义回调这样的东西需要更仔细地阅读（对我来说，还要反复试验）才能正确设置。 但我已经超越了自己，我将介绍实施中的问题。

我如何在 Express.js（和一点 React）中实现它们？

现在进入有趣的部分，如何在 Express/Node 应用程序中实现 Passport.js 和 JWT？ 老实说，它难倒了我好一阵子。 但经过大量教程、重新阅读文档并转向 Stack Overflow 寻求帮助后，我得到了理解和满足。

我应该说，我对这个应用程序的目标之一是使其模块化，使其添加或删除功能变得超级简单，所以当你看到我的文件结构时，你会注意到 CRUD 功能的所有路由（创建、读取、更新、删除）都内置在单独的文件中，并且所有 Passport 身份验证也都在一个集中的文件中处理。 这是我的应用程序 API 部分的文件结构。

这是package.json及其依赖项，因此您可以准确地看到我正在使用的内容。

package.json

 { " name " : " mysqlregistration-api " , " version " : " 1.0.0 " , " description " : "" , " main " : " index.js " , " scripts " : { " start " : " nodemon server.js --exec babel-node --presets es2015 " }, " repository " : { " type " : " git " , " url " : " git+https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration.git " }, " author " : " Paige Niedringhaus " , " license " : " MIT " , " bugs " : { " url " : " https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration/issues " }, " homepage " : " https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration#readme " , " dependencies " : { " babel-cli " : " ^6.26.0 " , " babel-preset-es2015 " : " ^6.24.1 " , " babel-preset-stage-0 " : " ^6.24.1 " , " bcrypt " : " ^3.0.2 " , " body-parser " : " ^1.18.3 " , " cors " : " ^2.8.4 " , " dotenv " : " ^6.1.0 " , " express " : " ^4.16.3 " , " helmet " : " ^3.20.1 " , " jsonwebtoken " : " ^8.3.0 " , " morgan " : " ^1.9.0 " , " mysql2 " : " ^1.5.3 " , " nodemailer " : " ^4.6.8 " , " nodemon " : " ^1.18.3 " , " passport " : " ^0.4.0 " , " passport-jwt " : " ^4.0.0 " , " passport-local " : " ^1.0.0 " , " sequelize " : " ^5.3.0 " , " sequelize-cli " : " ^4.0.0 " , " swagger-jsdoc " : " ^3.2.9 " , " swagger-ui-express " : " ^4.0.2 " } }

依赖项包括一些额外的东西，比如babel ，所以我可以在我的 Node.js 应用程序中使用 ES6 语法， bcrypt用于密码散列并作为我的 MySQL ORM jsonwebtoken sequelize passport 、 passport-local和passport-jwt 。 这些是这个博客的必需品。

我将首先从server.js文件开始，因为它需要的解释最少。 该文件纯粹用于启动服务器、初始化应用程序中 Passport 的使用以及设置路由和解析来自客户端的请求。

server.js

 import express from ' express ' ; import Cors from ' cors ' ; import bodyParser from ' body-parser ' ; import logger from ' morgan ' ; import passport from ' passport ' ; import helmet from ' helmet ' ; const app = express (); const API_PORT = process . env . API_PORT || 3000 ; require ( ' ./config/passport ' ); app . use ( Cors ()); app . use ( bodyParser . urlencoded ({ extended : false })); app . use ( bodyParser . json ()); app . use ( logger ( ' dev ' )); app . use ( helmet ()); app . use ( passport . initialize ()); require ( ' ./routes/loginUser ' )( app ); require ( ' ./routes/registerUser ' )( app ); require ( ' ./routes/findUsers ' )( app ); require ( ' ./routes/deleteUser ' )( app ); require ( ' ./routes/updateUser ' )( app ); // eslint-disable-next-line no-console app . listen ( API_PORT , () => console . log ( `Listening on port ${ API_PORT } ` )); module . exports = app ;

在imports和consts中有一点 ES6，但你应该关注的是passport.initialize()和routes 。

很简单吧？ 伟大的。 继续。

您可能还注意到了require('./config/passport'); 通常，这将作为环境变量存储在未签入到 Github 的文件中，但为了展示它是如何工作的，我在这里设置了它。

jwtConfig.js

 module . exports = { secret : ' jwt-secret ' , };

是的，所有这些都是passport.js文件的一部分。

我意识到这需要大量代码。如果您只想查看 Passport 的代码和带有 Passport 身份验证的路由，我在这里用代码创建了一些要点。

我将介绍这里发生的两种 Passport 实现：用于register和login方法的passport-local和用于jwt方法的passport-jwt 。

就像我之前说的，仅使用 Express.js 的文档都相当不错，但诀窍在于将它与 MySQL 数据库以及从前端的 React 客户端传递的信息相结合。

一旦passport-local策略通过了用户名和密码（我验证两个输入至少在客户端填写，然后再回调服务器），我在后端做的第一个检查是使用Sequelize ，我的SQL ORM（如 Mongoose for MongoDB），以确定该用户名是否存在于数据库中。 如果它返回null ，则身份验证失败（数据库中没有用户匹配），通常情况下， 401 Unauthorized HTTP 状态将从服务器返回到客户端而没有更多信息。

对我来说，这种类型的错误处理是 Passport 最令人沮丧的事情。 缺乏让用户（或我自己）知道实际错误超出401 Unauthorized的信息。

但是有一个更好的解决方案，需要一些额外的工作，使用自定义回调。 我将很快详细介绍回调，但现在，您可以查看是否存在某种错误，而不是返回return done(null, user); 然后可以将此消息从服务器传递回客户端，实际上告诉用户问题是什么（这是我想要的这个应用程序）。 如果您只想告诉用户他们的身份验证失败，但不透露原因，那也很酷。 您的选择，但我想展示如何完成错误消息传递。

现在这让我想到了名为jwt的passport-jwt策略。 这是在应用程序中的受保护路由上调用的身份验证： findUsers 、 updateUser 、 deleteUser 。

对于 jwt 策略，每次调用服务器时，JWT 都会从客户端传回（我在授权标头中传递了我的密钥： JWT ），然后使用密钥（存储在另一个文件中）对其进行提取和解码，但实际上应该是只有系统知道的环境变量）。

一旦 JWT 有效负载被解密，ID（对我来说就是用户名）可以像passport-local策略一样在数据库中搜索，或者返回done(null, user); 如果不是（这几乎永远不会发生，因为 JWT 以加密形式包含用户名，所以除非它被某种方式被篡改或数据库被篡改，否则它应该能够找到用户）。

这让我想到了我的第二个陷阱，这让我很长时间都被绊倒了。 并非所有护照策略都需要相同的解决方案。

如果您仔细观察，您会看到passport-local策略都已return done(null, user); 但是passport-jwt策略已经done (null, user); 看到不同？ 这是微不足道的，但是拥有（或删除该return ）是用户数据是否从中间件传回服务器之间的区别。

它让我的进度停滞了好几个小时，然后 Stack Overflow 的善意帮助我解决了这个问题（这是我第一次不得不向 SO 询问我已经找不到的答案，但它很好值得）。 因此，请注意何时return或不返回。

好的，所以我已经介绍了两种 Passport 策略和我在程序中用于中间件身份验证的三种方法，现在继续在服务器上进行身份验证之后。

下一步是如何在各种路由中实现这些新创建的方法。 这是registerUser路线。

registerUser.js

 import passport from 'passport'; import User from '../sequelize'; module.exports = app => { app.post('/registerUser', (req, res, next) => { passport.authenticate('register', (err, user, info) => { if (err) { console.error(err); } if (info !== undefined) { console.error(info.message); res.status(403).send(info.message); } else { req.logIn(user, error => { const data = { first_name: req.body.first_name, last_name: req.body.last_name, email: req.body.email, username: user.username, }; User.findOne({ where: { username: data.username, }, }).then(user => { user .update({ first_name: data.first_name, last_name: data.last_name, email: data.email, }) .then(() => { console.log('user created in db'); res.status(200).send({ message: 'user created' }); }); }); }); } })(req, res, next); }); };

正如你所看到的，这个 Passport 的实现看起来与大多数例子有点不同，这是因为我使用的是 Passport 的自定义回调版本，它需要一个闭包调用。 我正在使用它，以便在 Passport 的身份验证失败时传回的错误消息可以从服务器发送到客户端，而不是晦涩的401 Unauthorized 。

因为这是作为回调实现的（这就是为什么您在这些脚本中看到(req, res, next)不是一次，而是两次；它使我可以从 Passport 中间件访问(err, user, info) 。

信息是我感兴趣的——这是被发回的消息，所以如果信息不是null ，则意味着身份验证失败，然后我可以将消息发送给客户端，让他们知道原因。

现在，我来到第三个 Passport 陷阱，为了使闭包和自定义回调与passport-local一起工作，如果用户数据成功返回，则必须在处理user数据之前调用小方法req.logIn()中间件。

这在Passport 文档中有记录，但它的重要性并没有像我想的那样强调（我在最初几次试图让自定义回调工作时错过了它），这就是我现在强调它的原因。

它必须发生，一旦完成，我就可以从客户端获取额外的输入来注册用户，找到在passport-local register调用期间在数据库中创建的同一用户，并使用额外信息。 我也可以将这些额外的数据传递给中间件，但我希望 Passport 只处理身份验证，而不是用户创建。 模块化，记住。

另外，如果要将身份验证拆分为一个单独的服务，其中包含一个仅包含加密用户名和密码的单独数据库，那么这样做会更容易，然后使用用户名或 ID 来查找和更新此文件中的相应用户记录注册服务。

一旦所有这些都被成功处理，一个200 HTTP 状态和成功消息将被发送回客户端。

现在查看loginUser路由应该更有意义。

loginUser.js

 import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken'; import passport from 'passport'; import jwtSecret from '../config/jwtConfig'; import User from '../sequelize'; module.exports = app => { app.post('/loginUser', (req, res, next) => { passport.authenticate('login', (err, users, info) => { if (err) { console.error(`error ${err}`); } if (info !== undefined) { console.error(info.message); if (info.message === 'bad username') { res.status(401).send(info.message); } else { res.status(403).send(info.message); } } else { req.logIn(users, () => { User.findOne({ where: { username: req.body.username, }, }).then(user => { const token = jwt.sign({ id: user.id }, jwtSecret.secret, { expiresIn: 60 * 60, }); res.status(200).send({ auth: true, token, message: 'user found & logged in', }); }); }); } })(req, res, next); }); };

使用相同风格的自定义回调和闭包，最大的不同是一旦用户成功验证并定位到数据库中，使用jwt.sign(); 函数，它将用户名设置为在 JWT 中传递的 ID，并由我之前设置的秘密加密。

再一次，如果所有这些都成功，则会发送一个200 HTTP 状态，其中一个布尔值我命名为auth为客户端设置为 true，新生成的令牌和一个简短的登录成功消息。

最后但同样重要的是，这里是findUsers路线。 我对updateUser和deleteUser路由使用了相同的passport-jwt身份验证，因此我将仅以这个为例。

findUsers.js

 import passport from ' passport ' ; import User from ' ../sequelize ' ; module . exports = ( app ) => { app . get ( ' /findUser ' , ( req , res , next ) => { passport . authenticate ( ' jwt ' , { session : false }, ( err , user , info ) => { if ( err ) { console . log ( err ); } if ( info !== undefined ) { console . log ( info . message ); res . status ( 401 ). send ( info . message ); } else if ( user . username === req . query . username ) { User . findOne ({ where : { username : req . query . username , }, }). then (( userInfo ) => { if ( userInfo != null ) { console . log ( ' user found in db from findUsers ' ); res . status ( 200 ). send ({ auth : true , first_name : userInfo . first_name , last_name : userInfo . last_name , email : userInfo . email , username : userInfo . username , password : userInfo . password , message : ' user found in db ' , }); } else { console . error ( ' no user exists in db with that username ' ); res . status ( 401 ). send ( ' no user exists in db with that username ' ); } }); } else { console . error ( ' jwt id and username do not match ' ); res . status ( 403 ). send ( ' username and jwt token do not match ' ); } })( req , res , next ); }); };

我对所有经过 Passport 身份验证的路由都使用了自定义回调（我很欣赏良好、清晰的错误处理），所以这种代码风格现在应该看起来很常规。

这一次，当调用passport.authenticate()时，我实现了passport.js文件中定义的 JWT 策略。 相同的(err, user, info)从中间件传回，但这次没有来自req.logIn()的调用。

相反，我只是返回在身份验证期间找到的user对象，并将所有必需的字段连同auth布尔值和成功消息一起传递给客户端。 这让我想到了我的最后一个 Passport 陷阱。

这不完全是特定于 Passport 的问题，但它是另一件事让我绊倒。

正如我所说，我从一堆其他教程和文档中拼凑出我（现在更加完整）对 JWT 和 Passport 的理解，其中一个教程让我误入歧途。 除了真正的授权标头之外，它还让我将 JWT 传递回客户端，这是我选择让我的passport-jwt策略从客户端请求中提取 JWT 有效负载的方式。

出于这个原因，我想简要介绍一段前端代码，在那里我以正确的格式传回令牌，因此您不必像我一样浪费时间进行调试。

Profile.js

 async componentDidMount () { const accessString = localStorage . getItem ( ' JWT ' ); const { match : { params : { username }, }, } = this . props ; if ( accessString == null ) { this . setState ({ isLoading : false , error : true , }); } else { try { const response = await axios . get ( ' http://localhost:3003/findUser ' , { params : { username , }, headers : { Authorization : `JWT ${ accessString } ` }, }); this . setState ({ first_name : response . data . first_name , last_name : response . data . last_name , email : response . data . email , username : response . data . username , password : response . data . password , isLoading : false , error : false , }); } catch ( error ) { console . error ( error . response . data ); this . setState ({ error : true , }); } } }

我正在使用流行的基于 Promise 的 HTTP 客户端Axios来调用我的服务器，但您真正需要注意的是headers部分。

在调用服务器之前，我使用localStorage.getItem('JWT')从本地存储中提取 JWT（当我从服务器端的loginUser()路由转发令牌时，我使用 JWT 值设置的密钥） ，然后我使用headers: { Authorization: JWT ${accessString} }和一点 ES6 字符串插值来设置它。 这样，如果我的授权标头有多个要解析的值， passport-jwt仍然很容易通过查找与“JWT”关联的字符串来提取正确的 JWT 有效负载信息。

就是这样，我只是想覆盖它，因为它阻止了我一段时间，并且只有通过使用Insomnia进行 API 测试，我才能确认我的 JWT 令牌确实有效，并确定我从客户端错误地传递了授权标头。

结论

我知道我写了很多（并提供了一堆代码片段和要点），但身份验证不是一个简单的概念。 也不可能真正制作出如此安全的应用程序，任何人都无法破解它。 但是，如果您采取 JWT 和 Passport.js 身份验证中间件等预防措施，您的 Node.js 应用程序应该有更好的机会抵御恶意攻击。

请务必注意我在 Passport 中强调的问题——您将在前往更安全网站的路上。

几周后再回来看看——我会写更多关于 JavaScript、React、IoT 或其他与 Web 开发相关的东西。

感谢您的阅读，我希望这对您有所帮助，并让您更好地了解实现 Passport 身份验证和使用 JSON Web 令牌。

如果您想确保不会错过我写的文章，请在此处注册我的时事通讯： https ://www.paigeniedringhaus.substack.com

更多参考资料和资源



原文:

Introduction

Before you say it, I know, I know. There’s already a literal ton of MERN tutorials out there showing how to use JSON Web Tokens (JWT, pronounced "jot") and Passport.js with Express.

But here’s what every one of those tutorials failed to mention, and what mine will cover: how and why to use the various authentication flavors Passport offers (including passport-jwt), and the gotchas that tripped me up for hours on end as I put together a user registration application.

I don’t know what your experience has been, but the majority of the projects I’ve worked on professionally have come with authorization already prebuilt by someone else. So in addition to improving my MERN skills, I viewed this as an opportunity to learn more about security and authentication and different ways to do it.

This post will mainly cover how to implement of Passport and JWT on the server side, because that’s where most of the magic happens, but if you want, you can see the source files and download the full MERN project here.

Once it’s correctly hooked up on the backend, the frontend is purely React.

Before I jump into all that though, let me give you a quick run down of JWT and Passport.js authentication.

What is a JSON Web Token?

A JSON Web Token, according to the site is:

“…an open standard (RFC 7519) that defines a compact and self-contained way for securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object.” — JWT.io

Essentially, JWTs are digitally signed tokens that can be verified and trusted, and they’re becoming more and more popular for security and authorization between parties (like servers and clients) and for exchanging sensitive information, while verifying the token hasn’t been tampered with or decoded.

I won’t go into the details of how the tokens work, you can read all about that from a much more qualified source than I am here, but suffice it to say, I chose to use JWT as part of my user verification strategy along with Passport.js to make my simple user registration application secure (and because I was curious to see if I could do it, since I didn’t have a whole lot of hands on experience implementing authorization).

With that covered, the next piece of my authentication solution is Passport.js.

What is Passport.js & Why Should I Use it?

Passport.js is:

Simple, unobtrusive authentication for Node.js — Passport.js

And it works exceedingly well with Express.js. Passport is authentication middleware for Node, which serves one purpose, to authenticate requests, in a modular way that leaves all other functionality to the application itself, making code cleaner, easier to maintain and provides a clear separation of concerns.

If you type the term "JavaScript authentication middleware" or even just "JavaScript authentication" into the Google search bar, Passport.js ranks within the top 5 search results. That’s how ubiquitous this solution is in the JavaScript ecosystem.

Did I mention it boasts more than 500+ authentication strategies? It does. Whether you wish to log in with a simple username and password, with Github credentials, Facebook, oAuth, etc., there’s probably a Passport.js strategy for it.

So it was a simple decision to choose Passport as part of my authorization strategy.

I’d like to add documentation on the site is pretty good, though some things like custom callbacks require much more careful reading (and trial and error, for me) to correctly set up. But I’m getting ahead of myself, I’ll cover the gotchas in implementation.

How Do I Implement Them in Express.js (and a little React)?

Now on to the fun part, how do you implement both Passport.js and JWT into an Express/Node application? To be honest, it stumped me for a good while. But after numerous tutorials, re-readings of documentation and turning to Stack Overflow for help, I got to a place of understanding and satisfaction.

I should say, one of my goals for this app was to make it modular, make it super simple to add or remove functionality, so when you see my file structure, you’ll notice all the routes for the CRUD functionality (create, read, update, delete) are built into separate files, and all the Passport authentication is handled in one, centralized file as well. Here’s a look at the file structure of the API portion of my app.

The API File Tree 

root/
├── api/ 
| ├── config 
| | ├── passport.js 
| | ├── jwtConfig.js
| ├── server.js 
| ├── sequelize.js 
| ├── package.json
| ├── models/ 
| | ├── user.js
| ├── routes/ 
| | ├── deleteUser.js 
| | ├── findUsers.js 
| | ├── loginUser.js 
| | ├── registerUser.js
| | ├── updateUser.js
| ├── node-modules/

The package.json File

And here is the package.json and its dependencies so you can see exactly what I’m using.

package.json

{ "name": "mysqlregistration-api", "version": "1.0.0", "description": "", "main": "index.js", "scripts": { "start": "nodemon server.js --exec babel-node --presets es2015" }, "repository": { "type": "git", "url": "git+https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration.git" }, "author": "Paige Niedringhaus", "license": "MIT", "bugs": { "url": "https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration/issues" }, "homepage": "https://github.com/paigen11/mysqlRegistration#readme", "dependencies": { "babel-cli": "^6.26.0", "babel-preset-es2015": "^6.24.1", "babel-preset-stage-0": "^6.24.1", "bcrypt": "^3.0.2", "body-parser": "^1.18.3", "cors": "^2.8.4", "dotenv": "^6.1.0", "express": "^4.16.3", "helmet": "^3.20.1", "jsonwebtoken": "^8.3.0", "morgan": "^1.9.0", "mysql2": "^1.5.3", "nodemailer": "^4.6.8", "nodemon": "^1.18.3", "passport": "^0.4.0", "passport-jwt": "^4.0.0", "passport-local": "^1.0.0", "sequelize": "^5.3.0", "sequelize-cli": "^4.0.0", "swagger-jsdoc": "^3.2.9", "swagger-ui-express": "^4.0.2" }
}

The dependencies include a few extras like babel so I can use ES6 syntax in my Node.js app, bcrypt for password hashing and sequelize as my MySQL ORM, but the things you need to focus on are jsonwebtoken, passport, passport-local and passport-jwt. These are the necessities for this blog.

The server.js File

I’ll start with the server.js file first, as it requires the least explanation. This file is purely for starting the server, initializing the use of Passport in the app and setting up the routes and parsing of requests from the client side.

server.js

import express from 'express';
import Cors from 'cors';
import bodyParser from 'body-parser';
import logger from 'morgan';
import passport from 'passport';
import helmet from 'helmet'; const app = express(); const API_PORT = process.env.API_PORT || 3000; require('./config/passport'); app.use(Cors());
app.use(bodyParser.urlencoded({ extended: false }));
app.use(bodyParser.json());
app.use(logger('dev'));
app.use(helmet());
app.use(passport.initialize()); require('./routes/loginUser')(app);
require('./routes/registerUser')(app);
require('./routes/findUsers')(app);
require('./routes/deleteUser')(app);
require('./routes/updateUser')(app); // eslint-disable-next-line no-console
app.listen(API_PORT, () => console.log(`Listening on port ${API_PORT}`)); module.exports = app;

A little ES6 in the imports and consts, but what you should focus on is the passport.initialize() and the routes.

Pretty straightforward right? Great. Moving on.

You might also have noticed the require('./config/passport');, this is the next step I’ll go over. The JWT and Passport configuration, inside of the folder named config/.

The jwtConfig.js File

The JWT config is very simple, it’s the secret required by JWT to encode and decode the tokens. Typically, this would be stored as an environmental variable in a file that’s not checked in to Github, but to show how this works, I’ve set it in here.

jwtConfig.js

module.exports = { secret: 'jwt-secret',
};

The passport.js File

passport.js

import bcrypt from 'bcrypt';
import jwtSecret from './jwtConfig'; const BCRYPT_SALT_ROUNDS = 12; const passport = require('passport');
const LocalStrategy = require('passport-local').Strategy;
const JWTstrategy = require('passport-jwt').Strategy;
const ExtractJWT = require('passport-jwt').ExtractJwt;
const User = require('../sequelize'); passport.use( 'register', new LocalStrategy( { usernameField: 'username', passwordField: 'password', passReqToCallback: true, session: false, }, (req, username, password, done) => { try { User.findOne({ where: { [Op.or]: [ { username, }, { email: req.body.email }, ], }, }).then(user => { if (user != null) { console.log('username or email already taken'); return done(null, false, { message: 'username or email already taken', }); } bcrypt.hash(password, BCRYPT_SALT_ROUNDS).then(hashedPassword => { User.create({ username, password: hashedPassword, email: req.body.email, }).then(user => { console.log('user created'); return done(null, user); }); }); }); } catch (err) { return done(err); } }, ),
); passport.use( 'login', new LocalStrategy( { usernameField: 'username', passwordField: 'password', session: false, }, (username, password, done) => { try { User.findOne({ where: { username, }, }).then(user => { if (user === null) { return done(null, false, { message: 'bad username' }); } bcrypt.compare(password, user.password).then(response => { if (response !== true) { console.log('passwords do not match'); return done(null, false, { message: 'passwords do not match' }); } console.log('user found & authenticated'); return done(null, user); }); }); } catch (err) { done(err); } }, ),
); const opts = { jwtFromRequest: ExtractJWT.fromAuthHeaderWithScheme('JWT'), secretOrKey: jwtSecret.secret,
}; passport.use( 'jwt', new JWTstrategy(opts, (jwt_payload, done) => { try { User.findOne({ where: { id: jwt_payload.id, }, }).then(user => { if (user) { console.log('user found in db in passport'); done(null, user); } else { console.log('user not found in db'); done(null, false); } }); } catch (err) { done(err); } }),
);

Yes, all this is part of the passport.js file.

I realize this is a lot of code to take in. If you’d like to see just the code for Passport and the routes with Passport authentication, I created a few gists here with the code.

I’ll walk through the two types of Passport implementation happening here: passport-local for the register and login methods and passport-jwt for the jwt method.

passport-local uses a username and password, and passport-jwt uses a JWT payload to verify the user is legit.

Like I said earlier, the documentation for both using just Express.js is fairly good, but the trick comes in combining it with the MySQL database and the information passed from the React client on the frontend.

Once the passport-local strategy has been passed a username and password (which I verify that both inputs are at least filled on the client side before ever calling back to the server), the first check I do on the backend is with Sequelize, my SQL ORM (like Mongoose for MongoDB), to determine if that username exists in the database. If it returns null, the authentication fails (no user in the db matches), and typically, a 401 Unauthorized HTTP status would be thrown back from the server to the client with no further information.

Passport.js Gotcha #1: Info in Error Handling

This type of error handling, to me, was the most frustrating thing about Passport. The lack of information to let a user (or myself) know what the actual error was beyond 401 Unauthorized.

But there’s a better solution that requires a little extra work, with custom callbacks. I’ll go into more detail about the callback soon, but for now, you can see if there’s some sort of error, instead of returning a return done(null, user);, Passport can pass back return done(null, false, { message: 'bad username or passwords don't match' });.

This message can then be passed back from the server to the client, actually telling the user what the problem is (which is what I wanted for this app). If you prefer just to tell the user their authentication failed, but not disclose why, that’s cool too. Your choice, but I want to show how error messaging can be done.

The rest of the passport-local strategy is pretty self-explanatory, on register the user’s password is hashed and salted with the encryption package bcrypt, and then when the login method is invoked, it hashes the newly entered password and checks the passwords with bcrypt’s compare function before returning either a positive or negative on the verification.

Now that brings me to the passport-jwt strategy, which is named jwt. This is the authentication that’s called on the protected routes in the application: findUsers, updateUser, deleteUser.

For the jwt strategy, the JWT is passed back from the client with each call to the server (I passed mine in an authorization header with the key: JWT), which is extracted and then decoded using the secret (which is stored in another file, but should really be an environment variable known only to the system).

Once the JWT payload is decrypted, the ID (which for me is the username) can be searched for in the database just like the passport-local strategy does, and either returned with done(null, user);, if the user is found or with done(null, false); if it’s not (which should almost never happen because the JWT includes the username in its encrypted form, so unless that’s somehow been tampered with or the db has been, it should be able to find the user).

Passport.js Gotcha #2: Passport-Local Wants Return, Passport-JWT Does Not

This brings me to my second gotcha which tripped me up for a good bit of time; not all passport strategies require the same resolution.

If you’re looking closely, you’ll see the passport-local strategies both have return done(null, user); but the passport-jwt strategy has done (null, user);. See the difference? It’s minuscule, but having (or removing that return), is the difference between that user data being passed back from the middleware to the server or not.

And it halted my progress for a good few hours, before the kindness of Stack Overflow helped me work through the issue (it’s the first time I’ve actually had to ask SO for an answer I couldn’t find already, but it was well worth it). So be aware of when to return or not.

Ok, so I’ve walked through the two Passport strategies and the three methods I’m using for middleware authentication in my program, now on to after the authentication on the server.

The register.js File

The next step is how to implement these newly minted methods inside of the various routes. Here’s the registerUser route.

registerUser.js

import passport from 'passport';
import User from '../sequelize';

module.exports = app => {
  app.post('/registerUser', (req, res, next) => {
    passport.authenticate('register', (err, user, info) => {
      if (err) {
        console.error(err);
      }
      if (info !== undefined) {
        console.error(info.message);
        res.status(403).send(info.message);
      } else {
        req.logIn(user, error => {
          const data = {
            first_name: req.body.first_name,
            last_name: req.body.last_name,
            email: req.body.email,
            username: user.username,
          };
          User.findOne({
            where: {
              username: data.username,
            },
          }).then(user => {
            user
              .update({
                first_name: data.first_name,
                last_name: data.last_name,
                email: data.email,
              })
              .then(() => {
                console.log('user created in db');
                res.status(200).send({ message: 'user created' });
              });
          });
        });
      }
    })(req, res, next);
  });
};

As you can see, this implementation of Passport looks a little different than most examples out there, and it’s because I’m using the custom callback version of Passport, which requires a closure call. I’m using this so that the error message that’s passed back if Passport’s authentication fails can be sent from the server to the client instead of the obscure 401 Unauthorized.

Because this is being implemented as a callback (which is why you see (req, res, next) not once, but twice in these scripts; it gives me access to the (err, user, info) from the Passport middleware.

The info is what I’m interested in — that’s the message being sent back, so if the info is anything besides null, that means the authentication failed and I can then send the message to the client to let them know why.

Passport.js Gotcha #3: Don't Forget req.logIn() in Your Custom Callback

Now, I’m to the third Passport gotcha, in order to make the closures and custom callbacks work with passport-local, the little method req.logIn() must be called before the user data is handled if it comes back successfully from the middleware.

This is documented in the Passport documentation, but it’s importance is not stressed as much as I would like (and I missed it the first few times trying to get custom callbacks to work), which is why I highlight it now.

It must happen, and once that’s done, I’m able to take the extra inputs from the client side for registering a user, find that same user created in the database during the passport-local register call, and update the user file with the extra info. I could have passed this extra data through to the middleware as well, but I want Passport to only handle authentication, not user creation as well. Modularization, remember.

Plus, if the authentication were to be split out into a separate service with a separate database of just encrypted usernames and passwords, this would make it easier to do so, then use the username or ID to find and update the corresponding user record in this registration service.

Once all that’s been successfully taken care of, a 200 HTTP status and success message are sent back to the client.

The loginUser.js File

Seeing the loginUser route now should make more sense.

loginUser.js

import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken';
import passport from 'passport';
import jwtSecret from '../config/jwtConfig';
import User from '../sequelize';

module.exports = app => {
  app.post('/loginUser', (req, res, next) => {
    passport.authenticate('login', (err, users, info) => {
      if (err) {
        console.error(`error ${err}`);
      }
      if (info !== undefined) {
        console.error(info.message);
        if (info.message === 'bad username') {
          res.status(401).send(info.message);
        } else {
          res.status(403).send(info.message);
        }
      } else {
        req.logIn(users, () => {
          User.findOne({
            where: {
              username: req.body.username,
            },
          }).then(user => {
            const token = jwt.sign({ id: user.id }, jwtSecret.secret, {
              expiresIn: 60 * 60,
            });
            res.status(200).send({
              auth: true,
              token,
              message: 'user found & logged in',
            });
          });
        });
      }
    })(req, res, next);
  });
};

The same style of custom callbacks and closures are used, the biggest difference is that once the user is successfully verified and located in the database, the JWT token is generated using the jwt.sign(); function, which sets the username as the ID passed in the JWT, and encrypted by the secret I set earlier.

Once again, if all this works successfully, a 200 HTTP status is sent with a boolean I named auth set to true for the client side, the newly generated token and a short login success message.

The findUsers.js File

And last, but not least, here’s the findUsers route. I used the same passport-jwt authentication for the updateUser and deleteUser routes, so I’ll just show this one as an example.

findUsers.js

import passport from 'passport';
import User from '../sequelize'; module.exports = (app) => { app.get('/findUser', (req, res, next) => { passport.authenticate('jwt', { session: false }, (err, user, info) => { if (err) { console.log(err); } if (info !== undefined) { console.log(info.message); res.status(401).send(info.message); } else if (user.username === req.query.username) { User.findOne({ where: { username: req.query.username, }, }).then((userInfo) => { if (userInfo != null) { console.log('user found in db from findUsers'); res.status(200).send({ auth: true, first_name: userInfo.first_name, last_name: userInfo.last_name, email: userInfo.email, username: userInfo.username, password: userInfo.password, message: 'user found in db', }); } else { console.error('no user exists in db with that username'); res.status(401).send('no user exists in db with that username'); } }); } else { console.error('jwt id and username do not match'); res.status(403).send('username and jwt token do not match'); } })(req, res, next); });
};

I used custom callbacks for all my Passport authenticated routes (I appreciate good, clear error handling), so this code style should look routine by now.

This time, when passport.authenticate() is called, I implement the JWT strategy defined in the passport.js file. The same (err, user, info) gets passed back from the middleware, but there’s no call from req.logIn() this time.

Instead, I just return the user object found during the authentication and pass all the required fields to the client along with the auth boolean and success message. The JWT token is still stored in local storage on the client side so there’s no need to regenerate or pass it to the client again as well. Which brings me to my final Passport gotcha.

Passport.js Gotcha #4: Passing Authorization Headers Correctly

This one’s not exactly a Passport-specific gotcha, but it is yet another thing that tripped me up.

As I said, I pieced together my (now much more whole) understanding of JWT and Passport from a bunch of other tutorials and documentation, and one of those tutorials led me astray. It had me passing the JWT back to the client in something besides true authorization headers, which is the way that I chose to have my passport-jwt strategy extract the JWT payload from the client request.

For this reason, I wanted to touch briefly on one piece of the frontend code, where I pass back the token in the proper format, so you don’t have to waste the time debugging like I did.

Profile.js

 async componentDidMount() { const accessString = localStorage.getItem('JWT'); const { match: { params: { username }, }, } = this.props; if (accessString == null) { this.setState({ isLoading: false, error: true, }); } else { try { const response = await axios.get('http://localhost:3003/findUser', { params: { username, }, headers: { Authorization: `JWT ${accessString}` }, }); this.setState({ first_name: response.data.first_name, last_name: response.data.last_name, email: response.data.email, username: response.data.username, password: response.data.password, isLoading: false, error: false, }); } catch (error) { console.error(error.response.data); this.setState({ error: true, }); } } }

I’m using the popular promise-based HTTP client Axios to make my server calls, but what you really need to notice is the headers section.

Before the call to the server happens, I extract the JWT from local storage using localStorage.getItem('JWT') (the key I set with the JWT value when I passed the token forward from the loginUser() route on the server side), and then I set it using headers: { Authorization:JWT ${accessString}} and a little ES6 string interpolation. That way, if my authorization headers had more than one value to parse out, it would still be easy for passport-jwt to extract the correct JWT payload info by finding the string associated with "JWT".

And that’s it, I just wanted to cover it because it blocked me for a while and only through API testing with Insomnia, could I confirm my JWT token was actually working, and suss out that I was passing authorization headers from the client side incorrectly.

Conclusion

I know I wrote a lot (and provided a bunch of code snippets and gists), but authentication is not a simple concept. Nor is it truly possible to really make an app so secure no one will ever be able to hack it. But if you take precautions like JWT and Passport.js authentication middleware, your Node.js app should have a better chance to withstand people with malicious intent.

Just be sure to look out for the gotchas I highlighted around Passport — and you’ll be on your way to a more secure site.

Check back in a few weeks — I’ll be writing more about JavaScript, React, IoT, or something else related to web development.

Thanks for reading, I hope this proves helpful and gives you a better understanding of implementing Passport authentication and using JSON web tokens.

If you’d like to make sure you never miss an article I write, sign up for my newsletter here: https://www.paigeniedringhaus.substack.com

Further References & Resources
item.author: paigen11

item.title: 对区块链有清晰的认识 | Have a crystal clear understanding about a Blockchain
item.description:
对或错？

交易使用一个或多个输入 UTXO 指定的金额，并将其转移到一个或多个新创建的输出 UTXO。

它之所以得名，是因为它涉及“工作”或计算能力来解决难题并声称有权形成下一个区块。

对或错？

矿工承担额外的工作或计算来验证交易、广播交易、竞争创建区块的权利、通过验证区块、广播新创建的区块和确认交易来达成共识。

完全正确。

对或错？

外部拥有账户 (EOA) 和合同账户。

Q11- The _____ can send transactions for ether transfer or they can send transactions to invoke a smart contract code. Q11- _____ 可以发送交易以进行以太币转账，也可以发送交易以调用智能合约代码。

A11- accounts(address) A11-账户（地址）

Q12- What is the smallest denomination of cryptocurrency on Ethereum? Q12-以太坊上最小面额的加密货币是什么？

A12- Wei A12-魏

Q13- ____ hosts the software needed for transaction initiation, validation, mining, block creation, and smart contract execution. Q13- ____ 托管交易启动、验证、挖掘、区块创建和智能合约执行所需的软件。

A13- Ethereum full node A13-以太坊全节点

Q14- _____ receive, verify, gather and execute transactions. Q14- _____ 接收、验证、收集和执行交易。

A14- Miner nodes A14-矿工节点



原文:

Q1- What is blockchain composed of?

A1- A block is composed of a header of information about the block and a set of valid transactions.

Q2- What does UTXO stand for?

A2- Unspent Transaction Output.

Q3- Miners are computers that execute?

A3- Miners are powerful computer executing software defined by the Blockchain protocol.

Q4- A transaction generates new UTXOs for transferring the amount specified in the input UTXOs. True or False?

A4- True. The transaction uses the amount specified by one or more input UTXOs and transfers it to one or more newly created output UTXOs.

Q5- The algorithm for consensus in the Bitcoin blockchain is called _____ protocol.?

A5- Proof of Work (POW)
It gets its name because it involves “work” or computational power to solve the puzzle and to claim the right to form the next block.

Q6- Transaction confirmation is independently performed by all miner nodes. True or False?

A6- True
Miners, take on added work or computation to verify transactions, broadcast transactions, compete to claim the right to create a block, work on reaching consensus by validating the block, broadcasting the newly created block and confirming transactions.

Q7- Transaction 0 in every block in the bitcoin blockchain ____

A7- a) is for paying the miner fees b)is called the coinbase transaction c)does not have any input UTXO.

All are correct.

Q8 - What allows for the execution of code in the Ethereum Blockchain, while enhancing the basic value transfer capability of the Bitcoin Blockchain?

A8- Smart Contracts

Q9- Solidity has data, functions or methods with modifiers, along with getter and setter functions. True or False?

A9- True

Q10- How many types of accounts are on Ethereum?

A10- Two types of accounts. Externally Owned Accounts (EOA) and Contract Accounts.

Q11- The _____ can send transactions for ether transfer or they can send transactions to invoke a smart contract code.

A11- accounts(address)

Q12- What is the smallest denomination of cryptocurrency on Ethereum?

A12- Wei

Q13- ____ hosts the software needed for transaction initiation, validation, mining, block creation, and smart contract execution.

A13- Ethereum full node

Q14- _____ receive, verify, gather and execute transactions.

A14- Miner nodes
item.author: charliehexx

item.title: 为什么选择 Microsoft 以及我如何实现我的目标 | Why Microsoft and How I achieved my Goal
item.description:
但这很无聊，所以一年后我离开了，在马略卡岛的舞台上玩乐并成为一名艺人。 几年来我玩得很开心，但后来又开始学习，试图从 flash 开始重新回到技术领域，然后转向 wordpress。

在为当地的酒吧、餐馆、鱼贩和跆拳道俱乐部建立了许多网站之后，我终于被录用并开始为初创公司工作，担任专门从事 HTML 和 CSS 的前端开发人员。 我被解雇了很多次，因为每家初创公司都在不断地缺钱，所以我不断地进进出出工作。 生存非常艰难，所以我成为了一名英语老师，以便能够支付账单并尽可能做网络工作。

我的梦想 - 为一家很酷的科技公司工作

这一切都在 5 年前发生了变化，当时我的生活并没有真正走向任何地方，而且我非常痛苦。 那时我开始关注梦想建设者课程，这样我就可以改变我的生活。 我的梦想是作为前端开发人员为 Google 工作。 等一下你说，我还以为标题是“Why Microsoft?”。

让我解释。 在我生命中的那个时候，微软并不是我的梦想。 这不是我今天所知道的公司，所以这不是我的目标。 在我的梦想建设者课程中，我被告知要定义谷歌。 对我来说，这与酷办公室或免费食物或其他任何让 Google 酷的东西无关。 这是关于与一支优秀的团队合作，关于能够有所作为，关于始终能够学习和改进，我不会说谎，这也是关于为一家不成熟的公司工作。几年后不会倒闭。

获得 Microsoft 认证

离开我的教学工作和自由开发工作后，我于 2017 年 2 月回到全日制学习，学习 JavaScript，希望能在一家伟大的公司找到一份工作。 我从没想过我会被谷歌录用，但我总觉得你必须追求明星并尽可能高地瞄准。 经过大约 9 个月的全日制学习后，我于 2017 年 11 月在 Bluekiri 找到了一份真正的前端开发人员的工作。

这是事情发生变化的时候。 这家公司是微软的合作伙伴，CEO 鼓励每个人写博客文章、在会议上发言、在开源方面进行合作并获得微软认证，这并不容易，但我很自豪。

与 Microsoft Guys 构建聊天机器人

在那里工作时，我们的任务是在 Node.js 中构建聊天机器人。 我从来没有足够的经验，但被允许跟踪项目并为聊天机器人仪表板构建一些 UI。 开发人员和我们坐在一起，和我们一起编写聊天机器人，帮助我们更好地了解我们正在使用的产品。 这真好玩。

我们每天都和这些人共进午餐，最后一天我和他们一起坐出租车去机场，我记得和他们一起坐在机场里，手里拿着我准备获得微软认证的书，他们告诉我去机场是什么感觉为微软工作。 那是我的目标改变的时候。 我真的很喜欢微软愿意派人去帮助别人，帮助他们用他们的产品构建更好的软件的想法，我对微软的两个人说，

但这又只是一个目标，一个梦想。 一个人是怎么到达那里的？ 与往常一样，这需要时间。 很多时间。 那时我还不够好，无法为微软工作。 我需要更多的经验和技能。

你的目标是什么？

在 Bluekiri 工作一年后，我搬到了一家机构。 在 2018 年 10 月的面试中，我被问到了一个典型的问题。 你在 5 年后看到自己在哪里？ 你的目标是什么？ 我的回答很简单。 我说：

我将在微软工作。

现在想象一下。 一位一年前学习 JavaScript 的女性开发人员在小马略卡岛的一家小型机构的面试小组中告诉她，她将在微软工作，并以如此坚定和信念表示。 好吧，他们的下巴掉了下来，他们的眼睛睁得大大的。 我不认为我或他们认为这会发生，但我有一个目标，我的目标非常明确。

这成为我目标的一部分。 我需要做什么？ 我怎么能成为一个人？ 我已经在为开源做出贡献，帮助社区和分享知识，所以实际上只是做更多的事情，并试图帮助人们更多。

参观波士顿的微软办公室

我真的告诉​​我丈夫去购物，稍后见我。 对我来说，走进办公室并看到 Microsoft 徽标只是一个梦想。 我觉得我离梦想又近了一步。 在那次旅行之后，Jen Looper 提名我为 MVP 计划。

我的MVP奖

我真的不敢相信。 我最近还获得了谷歌开发者专家奖，因此被两家最出色的科技公司认可，这两家公司将是我的梦想，这简直是疯了。

被如此出色的演讲者和 MVP 包围着，真是令人难以置信的体验。 在那次航行中我成长了很多，这真的让我更加相信我可以实现我的目标。

第一届MVP峰会

我真的觉得我在某种程度上是微软的一部分。 我喜欢这些会议，走廊里的轨道令人惊叹，我们中的许多 MVP 只是在这些困难时期聊天、闲逛并相互支持。

成为 GitHub 明星

然而，我用我的时间专注于我的目标，尽我所能做的更好，我尽可能多地与他人分享。 这就是那年晚些时候我成为 GitHub Star 的原因。 我的信念始终是，如果我可以改变我的生活，那么其他人也可以。 他们只需要看到并相信他们可以。 因此，我与大家分享我的知识和学习，以便他们实现梦想。

作为 MVP 的合作

作为 MVP，我与微软的许多人进行了很多合作，这真是太棒了。 特别感谢 Simona Cotin 和 Wassim Chegham 一直相信我并给了我这些惊人的机会。

微软构建 2020

我必须在 2020 年与 Simona Cotin 和其他人一起出现在 Microsoft Build 上，在 Nuxt 和静态网站上进行了一次简短的采访，这是一次令人难以置信的经历。

我必须与 Simona Cotin 合作测试 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序的 Nuxt 集成和文档，并在 MS Build 上实时展示了我的演示。

微软创造

我与 Wassim Chegham 和 Burke Holland 一起出现在 MS Create 上，谈论为什么静态网站又回来了，从静态的服务器浏览器到静态的。

一个开发问题

我为微软在 Nuxt.js 上的一个开发问题创建了一个迷你 Twitter 视频系列。 全部远程记录了使用 Nuxt 和 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序的许多原因。

微软启动开发人员

我在 Microsoft Startup Developers 博客上发表了一篇关于我们如何在 Nuxt.js 公司使用 Nuxt 的文章。

微软云技能

我还出现在 JavaScript 社区的 Microsoft 云技能秀上，我们与 Wassim Chegham、Yohan Lasorsa 和 Ana Cidre 讨论了 JavaScript 框架的介绍。

有明确的目标

这些都是我有一天实现目标的微小步骤，但这些机会也让我更加意识到微软是一家多么酷的公司。

我再次非常自信地告诉他，

我将在微软工作。

当我把通知交给他时，我问他是否记得我的回答，他说：“是的，微软”。

那么它是怎么发生的呢？ 我想那是正确的时间和正确的地点。 有点运气，但也要做好准备。 心中有一个目标，并为之奋斗，直到出现合适的机会。 您可以在我的采访大型科技公司的帖子中阅读所有相关信息。

我的愿望实现了

我仍然不敢相信我真的得到了这份工作。 我仍然在高处，漂浮在云端。 一个人能取得的成就真是令人难以置信。



原文:

I started building websites in 1997 before I even had access to the internet. I got my first job in tech in 1999 working with Dreamweaver. But it was very boring so I left after a year to have fun and be an entertainer on a stage in Mallorca. I had fun for a few years but then started studying again to try to get back into tech starting with flash and then moving on to wordpress.

After building many websites for the local bars, restaurants, fish mongers and Taekwondo clubs, I finally got hired and started working for startups as a front-end developer specializing in HTML and CSS. I was let go many times as each startup kept running out of money and so I was constantly in and out of work. It was very hard to survive, so I became an English teacher to be able to pay the bills and did web work when I could.

My Dream - To work for a Cool Tech Company

It all changed 5 years ago when my life was not really going anywhere and I was pretty miserable. That's when I stared to do a dream builders course so I could change my life. My dream was to work for Google as a frontend developer. Wait a second you say, I thought this was titled "Why Microsoft?".

Let me explain. At that time in my life Microsoft was not my dream. It wasn't the company that I know it to be today, so it wasn't my goal. During my dream builders course I was told to define Google. For me it was never about the cool offices or free food or whatever else makes Google cool. It was about working with a great team of people, it was about being able to make a difference, it was about always being able to learn and improve, and I won't lie in saying it was also about working for a company that wasn't going to close down after a few years.

Becoming Microsoft Certified

After leaving my teaching job and freelance dev work I went back to full time study in February 2017, to learn JavaScript, in the hope to get a job at a great company. I never thought I would ever get hired by Google but I always feel you have to reach for the stars and aim as high as possible. After about 9 months of full time studying I got a job as a real frontend developer in Bluekiri in November of 2017.

This is when things changed. This company were Microsoft Partners and the CEO encouraged everyone to write blog posts, speak at conferences, collaborate on Open Source and be Microsoft certified, an achievement that was not easy, but that I am very proud of.

Building Chat Bots with the Microsoft Guys

While working there we were tasked to build chat bots in Node.js. I in no way had close to enough experience, but was allowed to shadow the project and build some of the UI for the chat bots dashboard. Microsoft sent us 2 people to help us build these chat bots. The developers sat with us and programmed the chat bots with us and helped us better understand the product we were using. It was so much fun.

We had lunch with these guys every day and on the last day I shared a taxi with them to the airport and I remember sitting in the airport with them with my book on prepping to be Microsoft certified, and they told me what it was like to work for Microsoft. And that is when my goals changed. I just really liked the idea that Microsoft were willing to send people out to help others, to help them build better software with their products and I said to the two Microsoft guys,

"One day I will work for Microsoft".

But again it was just a goal, a dream. How does one ever even get there? As always, it takes time. A lot of time. At that moment I was no way good enough to work for Microsoft. I needed a lot more experience and skills.

What are your goals?

After a year working for Bluekiri I moved to an agency. During my interview in October 2018, I was asked the typical question. Where do you see yourself in 5 years time? What are your goals? My answer was simple. I said:

I will be working for Microsoft.

Now picture it for a second. A female developer who learnt JavaScript a year ago, is telling an interview panel in a small agency, in the small island of Mallorca, that she is going to work for Microsoft and says it with such determination and belief. Well their jaws dropped and their eyes opened wide. I don't think either I or them thought it would happen but I had a goal and my goal was very clear.

The MVP Program

In November 2018 I spoke at my first conference and got to meet speakers who were Microsoft Most Valuable Professionals, and they started telling me about the MVP program. This became part of my goal. What did I need to do? How could I become one? I was already contributing to open source, helping communities and sharing knowledge so really it was just about doing more of it and trying to help people even more.

Visiting the Microsoft Office in Boston

While on holiday in Boston, in November 2019, I went to the Microsoft offices to help Jen Looper mentor a group of women who were learning to code. I literally told my husband to go shopping and meet me later. For me it was just a dream walking into the offices and seeing the Microsoft logo. I felt like I was taking a step closer to my dream. And after that trip is when Jen Looper nominated me for the MVP program.

My MVP Award

On the 1st January 2020 I received the email to say I had been accepted into the program and I was blown away. I really couldn't believe it. I had also recently been awarded the Google Developer Expert award and so to be acknowledged by two of the most amazing tech companies, two companies that it would be a dream to work for, was just insane.

In January 2020 I got to travel on a cruise ship for 17 days with a bunch of speakers and MVPs to give a tech talk in the Antarctic waters. It was such an incredible experience to be surrounded by such amazing speakers and MVPs. I grew so much during that cruise and it really helped me believe even more that I could accomplish my goals.

First MVP Summit

In March 2020 it was my first MVP summit, which I was meant to attend in person, but at last minute it was moved to be remote due to Covid, but I still really enjoyed it. I really felt I was part of Microsoft in a way. I loved the sessions and the hallway tracks were amazing with many of us MVPs just chatting and hanging out and supporting each other in these difficult times.

Becoming a GitHub Star

In April 2020 I started working for Nuxt, which was just incredible, but also my first job as a Developer Advocate in Covid times made things rather different. However I used my time to focus on my goals, to do everything I could to just be better at what I did, and I shared as much as I could with others. And that's how later that year I became a GitHub Star. My belief is always, if I could change my life then others can too. They just have to see and believe that they can. So I share my knowledge and learnings with everyone so they can make their dreams come true.

Collaborations as an MVP

As an MVP I got to collaborate a lot with many people from Microsoft which was just amazing. Special thanks to Simona Cotin and Wassim Chegham for always believing in me and for giving me these amazing opportunities.

Microsoft Build 2020

I got to appear on Microsoft Build in 2020 alongside Simona Cotin and others for a short interview on Nuxt and static sites which was an incredible experience.

Nuxt and Azure Static Web Apps MS Build

I got to collaborate with Simona Cotin on testing the Nuxt integration and docs for Azure Static Web Apps and had my demo shown live on MS Build.

MS Create

I appeared on MS Create with Wassim Chegham and Burke Holland talking about why static sites were back, from static, servers browsers to static.

One Dev Question

I created a mini Twitter video series for Microsoft's One Dev Question on Nuxt.js. All recorded remotely on the many reasons to use Nuxt and Azure Static Web Apps.

Microsoft Startup Developers

I had an article published on the Microsoft Startup Developers blog on how we use Nuxt at the Nuxt.js Company.

Microsoft Cloud Skills

I also appeared on the Microsoft Cloud Skills show on JavaScript Communities where we got to talk about an intro to JavaScript Frameworks, with Wassim Chegham, Yohan Lasorsa and Ana Cidre.

Having Clear Goals

These were all my tiny steps to one day achieve my goal, but also these opportunities made me realize even more what a cool company Microsoft is.

In February 2021 I interviewed for Bit and the CEO asked me what my 3 year plan was, what my goal was. And I told him, again with extreme confidence,

I will be working for Microsoft.

And when I handed in my notice to him, I asked him if he remembered my answer and he said, "yes, Microsoft".

So how did it happen? I guess it was right time and right place. A little bit of luck but also being prepared. Having a goal in mind and just going for it until the right opportunity arose. You can read all about it in my post Interviewing with the Big Tech Companies.

My dream came true

I still can't quite believe I actually got the job. I'm still on a high and floating in the clouds. 5 years ago I used all my life savings to study and learn JavaScript and now at 43 years of age (yes I know you all think I am only 21) I get to work for Microsoft. It really is just incredible what one can achieve.
item.author: debs_obrien

item.title: 前端大师 | Front End Masters
item.description: 原文:
item.author: martinxo

item.title: Python 与 Java：比较优缺点和用例 | Python vs. Java: Comparing the Pros, Cons, and Use Cases
item.description:
最初由 Adam Stempniak 撰写

有些战斗似乎无法取胜，而且显然一直在肆虐。

星球大战与星际迷航。 猫与狗。 苹果与三星。

但是当涉及到软件开发时，往往会出现不同的冲突：

质量与时间。 时间与成本。 成本与质量。

这些问题在为您的项目选择编程语言方面起着关键作用，这是您必须做出的第一个重大决定之一。

作为一家 Python 软件公司，我们非常熟悉将Python 与其他语言进行对比的挑战：

在本文中，我们将重点关注最后一个。

当然，这样的比较并不像看起来那样一成不变。 它通常比简单明了的利弊清单要复杂一些。

例如，用 Java 构建一个 MVP 可能需要几个月的时间，而Python在几周内就能得到相同的结果。 然而，Java 仍然受到大银行和金融科技公司的欢迎。 他们的选择是否合理？

这个故事还有很多。 每种语言都有不同的用例，当你做出选择时，你应该看看对你最重要的东西。

事不宜迟，让我们详细分析一下 Python 与 Java 的比较。

解释性语言在程序执行命令时将人类可读的代码转换为机器可读的代码，使其更容易修改或调试。

相反，编译语言必须在运行前将源代码翻译成机器代码，这使得代码更难修改或调试。

更重要的是， Python 是动态类型的，而 Java 是静态类型的。

尽管代码翻译与类型检查无关，但它们的定义是彼此的镜像：动态类型意味着在运行时检查类型，而静态类型意味着在执行之前检查类型。

由此产生的差异在编译时间上很明显。 一般来说，我们可以说Python 启动速度快，运行速度慢，而 Java 启动速度慢，运行速度快。

这也是灵活性和可靠性之间的冲突，分别。 我的一位同事曾经说过一句话让我印象深刻：

虽然有些夸张，但这种说法与事实相去甚远。

不过，快速入门 Python 只是一方面。 学习如何很好地使用它需要更多的时间。

使用Java，有一个明确的学习曲线，并且入门点很高。 开始编写并熟悉它需要花费大量时间，并且每个 API 都是不同的故事。

但是，一旦您的开发人员投入工作，您将从第一天开始就获得更高质量的代码。 因此，Java 要求的时间花得很好，但需要一段时间才能看到结果。

简而言之，开始使用 Python 需要数周时间，而开始使用 Java 需要数月时间。

该语言的设计方式是一切都需要从一开始就定义。

这意味着您显然需要更多时间来审查用 Java 编写的代码，因为它的内容更多——更不用说修复您可能发现的所有潜在问题了。 当它足够糟糕时，实际上最好从头开始重写整个东西，而不是花费时间和金钱来调试它。

但是这个代码量不一定是坏事。

当然，你拥有的代码越多，它就越复杂——但如果你写得好，你就会得到更健壮、更稳定、崩溃更少的软件。 对于小型项目来说，这可能并不重要，但对于处理大量各种数据的大型项目来说，情况就完全不同了。

这种感知的稳定性是大公司将 Java 视为一种强大的语言的原因，它可以赋予他们秩序和安全性。 像银行或金融科技企业这样的大玩家通常不会三思而后行，因为 Java 看似出众的稳定性，而不考虑其他选择。

虽然 Java 可能传统上是企业的首选语言，但我们应该问自己：我们真的可以说 Java 是企业解决方案吗？

不完全的。 否则，Dropbox、Instagram 或谷歌等科技巨头不会选择它作为他们的技术堆栈。 实际上，在金融科技中使用 Python 是一个特别有力的案例。 总而言之，说 Python 给你一个不稳定的产品是不准确的。

那么为什么会有 Java 更适合企业的先入之见呢？

除了纯粹的代码量（这本身并不总是一个优势）之外， Java 被视为对企业友好，因为它享有强大的库支持。

示例包括：

图书馆很重要。 这与 Python 对 AI/ML 友好的原因相同——稍后会详细介绍。

底线是性能是众多因素的总和，主要涉及您的代码环境和外部支持。 话虽如此，稳定性规模略微倾向于 Java。

如果您时间紧迫并希望赶上最后期限，您应该选择 Python。

使用 Python 编写的中等质量代码构建 MVP，然后再对其进行重构是一个非常有效的解决方案。 有时，你甚至会先做一个模型，看看你的产品是如何工作的，然后再决定实际的语言。 时间绝对有利于 Python。 使用 Java 构建 MVP 可能需要几个月的时间，而使用 Python 可以在几周内达到该阶段。

与此一致，Java 项目通常需要数年时间——一个长达一年的项目被认为是小项目——而对于 Python，拥有只需要几个月工作的项目是完全正常的。

更重要的是， Java 通常需要更大的开发团队。 由于所有这些原因， Python 是初创公司的绝佳选择。 如果尽可能快地开发 MVP 是您的首要任务，那么 Python 不会让您失望。

这是一项相当大的投资。

对于大多数用例，它非常适合。

请注意，仅仅因为 Java 更稳定且更昂贵并不意味着 Python 不可靠或更低层的语言。 离得很远。

用 Java 编写一些项目可能是矫枉过正——形式重于内容，如果你愿意的话——但是有资源的大公司经常选择它而不是其他语言，仅仅是因为它是更昂贵的解决方案，因此在他们看来更好。

那里的逻辑值得商榷，但并不是那些公司因此而受苦。

如果您有足够的时间和可支配的预算，那么没有理由不使用 Java。 你最终会得到一个与 Python 质量相当的产品，尽管你的开发会更长更慢。

没有两种方法：在趋势技术方面，Python 无可匹敌。

用 Java 编写任何一种都需要更多的代码，这会减慢开发速度并在流程中失去手头任务的重点。

但是 Python 被采用作为趋势技术的首选解决方案的主要原因是它提供了强大的支持，其中包括各种现成的库。 无论您追求什么技术上的新奇事物，都有一个 Python 工具可以帮助您。

更快地实现 AI 或 ML 的竞赛只是在加快速度，有种种迹象表明，Python 不仅会保持领先地位，而且会继续变得更加流行和广泛使用。

如果您的首要任务是开发速度，请使用 Python； 如果您的首要任务是稳定性高于一切，请使用 Java。

一般来说，Java 更适合处理复杂的任务。 另一方面，Python 更清晰、更容易、更简单——易于阅读、编写和修改。

请记住，这是一种过度简化。 您的选择应始终取决于您的个人需求、预算和正在进行的项目类型。

但是，如果当前的趋势继续下去并且该语言继续流行，那么可以肯定的是：Python 是未来。

这是否意味着我们很快就会看到一种语言取得胜利，而另一种语言逐渐消失的那一天？

不太可能。

更有可能的是，这场战斗将持续数年，就像许多其他同类一样。

由您决定：您站在哪一边？



原文:

Originally written by Adam Stempniak

Some battles seem unwinnable and have apparently been raging forever.

Star Wars vs. Star Trek. Cats vs. dogs. Apple vs. Samsung.

But when it comes to software development, different conflicts tend to come up:

Quality vs. time. Time vs. cost. Cost vs. quality.

These concerns play a key role in choosing the programming language for your project, which is one of the first major decisions you have to make.

As a Python software house, we are intimately familiar with the challenge of contrasting Python with other languages:

Python vs. Golang. Python vs. Node.js. Python vs. Java.

In this article, we’ll focus on the last one.

Granted, such comparisons aren’t as set in stone as they may appear. It’s usually a little more complicated than a cut-and-dry list of pros and cons.

For example, building an MVP in Java can take months, while Python gets you the same results in weeks. And yet, Java is still popular with big banks and fintechs. Are they justified in their choice?

There’s a lot more to the story. Each language has different use cases, and you should look at what matters most to you when you make your choice.

Without further ado, let’s break down in detail how Python compares to Java.

1. Interpreted vs. compiled and dynamic vs. static

The differences between Python and Java start at the most basic level.

Python is an interpreted language, while Java is a compiled language.

Interpreted languages convert human-readable code to machine-readable code on the go, as the program executes commands, making it easier to revise or debug.

Conversely, compiled languages must translate source code into machine code before run time, making the code harder to revise or debug.

What’s more, Python is dynamically typed, while Java is statically typed.

Even though code translation has nothing to do with type-checking, their definitions are mirror images of one another: dynamic typing means checking types during run time, while static typing means checking types before execution.

The resulting difference is noticeable in compilation time. Generally speaking, we could say that Python launches faster and runs slower, while Java launches slower and runs faster.

It’s also a conflict between flexibility and reliability, respectively. Python and Java have opposing approaches to when errors are detected and how strict each language is about allowing variables to change types.

2. Entry point: Python gets you started faster

One of my colleagues once said something that stuck with me:

“You can learn the basics of Python over a weekend and start coding.”

While slightly exaggerated, this statement isn’t far from the truth.

Python has a low entry point and is very user-friendly, making it the perfect choice for junior developers and programming newcomers.

Getting started on Python quickly is only one side of the coin, though; it takes much more time to learn how to use it well.

Python can give you the same functionalities as Java, but only if your developers have more experience in it, which can be inconvenient in some cases.

With Java, there is a definite learning curve and the entry point is high. It takes a lot of time to start writing in it and get to know it well, and each API is a different story.

However, once your developers put in the work, you will end up with higher-quality code from day one. So the time Java demands is well spent, but it will take a while before you see the results.

In short, it takes weeks to get started on Python, and months to get started on Java.

3. Stability: Java is slightly more stable

Java always requires more code. The language is designed in such a way that everything needs to be defined from the get-go.

This means you obviously need more time to review code written in Java, because there’s simply more of it—not to mention fixing all the potential issues you may find. When it’s bad enough, you’re actually better off rewriting the whole thing from scratch, rather than burning time and money to debug it all.

But this code volume doesn’t necessarily have to be a bad thing.

Certainly, the more code you have, the more complicated it gets—but if you write it well, you get more robust and stable software that crashes less. It may not matter as much for smaller-scale projects, but it’s a very different story for large ones that process a lot of data of all kinds.

This perceived stability is the reason large companies see Java as a strong language that gives them order and security. Big players like banks or fintech businesses usually don’t think twice before settling on Java due to its seemingly superior stability, without considering other options.

While Java may have traditionally been the go-to language for corporations, we should ask ourselves: can we really say that Java is the enterprise solution?

Not quite. Python is also well equipped to handle large-scale software products; otherwise, tech giants like Dropbox, Instagram, or Google wouldn’t have chosen it for their tech stack. Actually, there’s a particularly strong case to be made for using Python in fintech. All in all, it would be inaccurate to say that Python gives you an unstable product.

So why the preconception that Java is better for corporations?

Other than sheer code volume—which isn’t always an advantage in and of itself—Java is seen as enterprise-friendly because of the strong library support it enjoys.

Java offers plenty of libraries that help you perform various tasks common in enterprise applications. Examples include:

Libraries matter. It’s the same reason Python is AI/ML-friendly—more on that later.

The bottom line is that performance is a sum total of a plethora of factors, mostly involving your code environment and external support. That being said, the stability scale is slightly tipped in Java’s favor.

4. Speed: Python is perfect for building an MVP fast

Python is known for its speed and famously easy to write in, making development really fast. If you’re pressed for time and looking to meet a deadline, you should go with Python.

Building an MVP with medium-quality code written in Python, then refactoring it later is a perfectly valid solution. Sometimes, you even start off by doing a mockup to see how your product is going to work before you decide on the actual language. Python is an ideal choice for that purpose.

Time is decidedly in favor of Python. It can take months to build an MVP with Java, while with Python you can get to that stage in a matter of weeks.

In line with this, Java projects usually take years—a year-long project is considered small—while for Python it is perfectly normal to have projects that require only months of work.

What’s more, Java usually requires larger development teams. Python demands fewer developers, and sometimes even one will suffice, helping you lower the total cost of your project.

For all of these reasons, Python is a great choice for startups. If developing an MVP as fast as possible is your top priority, Python won’t let you down.

5. Resources: Java requires a larger investment than Python

Another reason why Java is considered the language of corporations, is because development in it demands a large budget and a lot of time. It’s a sizable investment all around.

Python is more cost-effective than Java, which is why small- and medium-sized projects prefer it. For most use cases, it’s a perfect fit.

Mind you, just because Java is more stable and expensive doesn’t mean Python is unreliable or a lower tier language. Far from it.

Writing some projects in Java can be overkill—form over content, if you will—but large companies with resources to spare often choose it over other languages simply because it is the pricier solution, and thus better in their eyes.

The logic there is debatable, but it’s not like those corporations suffer for it.

If you have plenty of time and a generous budget at your disposal, there’s no reason not to go with Java. You will end up with a product of highly comparable quality to Python, though your development will be longer and slower.

6. Trending technologies: Python is the best choice for AI/ML

There are no two ways about it: Python has no equal when it comes to trending technologies.

The easy architecture of Python makes it a perfect fit for artificial intelligence, while Python’s simplicity and clarity gives it the necessary edge over other languages to effectively design the complex internal logic of machine learning.

Writing either in Java would require much more code, slowing down development and losing focus of the task at hand in the process.

But the main reason Python has been adopted as the go-to solution for trending technologies is the strong support it offers with its wide variety of ready-made libraries. Whatever technical novelty you’re after, there’s a Python tool out there to help you out.

The race for quicker implementation of AI or ML is only picking up speed, and there’s every indication that Python will not only stay in the leader’s seat, but also continue to become more popular and widely used.

7. Key takeaways

If your top priority is development speed, go with Python; if your top priority is stability over all else, go with Java.

Generally speaking, Java is better equipped to handle complex tasks. On the other hand, Python is clearer, easier, and simpler—to read, write, and modify.

Keep in mind that this is an oversimplification. Your choice should always depend on your individual needs, your budget, and the type of project you have in the works.

However, if the current trends continue and the language keeps growing in popularity, one thing is certain: Python is the future.

Does that mean we will soon see a day when one language emerges victorious, and the other fades into obscurity?

Unlikely.

What’s far more likely is that the battle will carry on for years to come, like so many others of the same kind.

It’s up to you to decide: which side are you on?
item.author: ad_przewozny

item.title: 作为 .NET 开发人员从 Visual Studio 迁移到 VS Code | Moving from Visual Studio to VS Code as a .NET Developer
item.description:
像大多数开发人员一样，我一直使用 Visual Studio 进行 C# 开发。 但是在 VS Code 的崛起和 .NET Core 的操作系统不可知论之间，感觉就像是时候进行切换了。

我使用 Visual Studio 近十年了，我认为它可能是最强大的 IDE。 它具有大量的功能，并在“幕后”处理大量配置和命令，而不会迫使开发人员浪费时间处理这些事情。

但是，当事情发生故障时，这种类型的自动化会变得非常令人沮丧。 例如，当你意识到 Visual Studio 正在使用它自己的 Angular 版本时，它存储在你的系统文件中的某个位置，而微软决定让这变得很麻烦。

我承认这种类型的自动化对于开始使用新堆栈非常有用，但是现在我宁愿牺牲一些时间来处理配置，如果这意味着我可以获得更多控制权。 此外，我真的不喜欢被迫在 Windows 上开发。

这就是我转行的原因。 不是因为我可以将我的 VS Code 设置为初音未来主题。 这当然不是原因。

您将需要Microsoft 的 C# 扩展来处理 VS Code 中与 C# 相关的所有内容，包括附加到进程和调试。 还有智能感知。 没有那个就活不下去。

解决方案文件？

解决方案特定于 Visual Studio，在 VS Code 中没有位置。 相反，VS Code 使用可以包含类似于解决方案的项目组的工作区。 在这种情况下，它们被称为“多根”工作空间。

在 VS Code 项目中，您可以在 .vscode/launch.json 和 .vscode/tasks.json 文件中为每个项目定义启动配置和其他任务。 可以在 Workspace 文件中使用相同的配置格式来定义一次启动多个项目的行为。

由于 Workspace 文件也可以访问在项目级别定义的启动配置，因此使用起来非常好且模块化。 我通常做的是为我的 Workspace 中的每个项目定义所有单独的启动配置，然后在 Workspace 级别使用复合启动配置来同时调用许多项目启动配置。

工作区结构

我发现将每个项目保存在自己单独的存储库中，然后创建一个独立的存储库来存储 Workspace 文件是最实用的。 另一种方法是将工作区文件保存在“最重要”的存储库中，并使用对其他项目的相对引用，但我不喜欢这样做，因为它要求使用“重要”存储库，即使在以下情况下你不一定需要它。



原文:

Like most developers, I've always used Visual Studio for C# development. But between VS Code's rise to power and .NET Core's OS agnosticism, it feels like the time for a switch.

I've been using Visual Studio for nearly a decade and think it is probably the most powerful IDE available. It has an enormous set of features and handles mountains of configurations and commands "behind the scenes," without forcing the developer to waste time dealing with these things.

However, this type of automation becomes very frustrating when things break down. For instance, when you realize that Visual Studio is using its own version of Angular stored somewhere in your system files, and that Microsoft decided to make this a hassle to change...

I'll admit that this type of automation is extremely useful for getting started with a new stack, but these days I'd rather sacrifice a bit of time dealing with configurations if it means I'm given more control. Also I don't really like being forced to develop on Windows.

This is the reason I'm switching. Not because I can set my VS Code to be Hatsune Miku themed. That's certainly not the reason why.

C# Extension

You're going to need Microsoft's C# Extension to handle everything C# related within VS Code, including attaching to processes and debugging. Also Intellisense. Can't live without that.

Solution Files?

Solutions are specific to Visual Studio and have no place in VS Code. Instead, VS Code uses Workspaces which can contain groups of projects similar to Solutions. In this case, they're called "multi-root" workspaces.

Within a VS Code project, you can define launch configurations and other tasks for each project within the .vscode/launch.json and .vscode/tasks.json files. The same configuration format can be used within the Workspace file to define the behavior for launching multiple projects at once.

Since the Workspace file can access launch configurations defined on the project level as well, things are very nice and modular to work with. What I usually do is define all of the individual launch configurations for each project within my Workspace, and then use compound launch configurations at the Workspace level to call many of the project launch configs simultaneously.

Workspace Structure

I've found it the most practical to keep each project in their own separate repository, and then create an independent repo for storing the Workspace files. An alternative is to keep your workspace files in the "most important" repository, and use relative referencing to the other projects, but I'm not a fan of this since it mandates the use of the "important" repository, even in cases where you don't necessarily need it.
item.author: cppshane

item.title: 水合作用和您正在建立的体验 | Hydration and the experience you are building
item.description:
水合是计算机编程中使用的一个术语，通常用于讨论用数据填充对象的过程。

当仅将某些字段加载到对象中时，对象会部分水合。 人们可能不想加载不会被使用的数据，以避免浪费带宽、安全隐患、CPU 周期加载、传输和设置例程。 当涉及到 Web 开发时，通常意味着使用功能来固定加载的标记，使用客户端 JavaScript将应用程序状态和交互性添加到服务器呈现的 HTML中。

水化（或再水化）是一种技术，其中客户端 JavaScript通过将事件处理程序附加到 HTML 元素，将通过静态托管或服务器端呈现交付的静态 HTML 网页转换为动态网页。 必须从经验的角度考虑权衡取舍。

服务器渲染是一种避免客户端上数据获取和模板的额外往返的方法。 页面的完整 HTML 在服务器上生成以响应导航。 服务器渲染产生：[1]

  • 快速首次绘制 (FP)
  • 快速首次内容绘制 (FCP)
  • 快速互动 (TTI)

这是可能的，因为您只是向用户的浏览器发送文本和链接，因此它适用于各种设备和网络条件。

听起来很有趣，对吧？ 更快的 TTI 意味着用户可以尽快使用完全交互的页面。 但是，与开发中的许多其他事物一样，我们必须考虑权衡取舍。

这种方法的主要缺点：在服务器上生成页面需要时间，这通常会导致首字节时间 (TTFB) 变慢。 客户端渲染依赖于浏览器使用 JavaScript 完全渲染页面。 所有逻辑、数据获取、模板和路由都在客户端而不是服务器上处理。 在 [1] Server-Side Rendering、Client-Side Rendering 和 Rehydration 中提供的术语定义在 Rendering 上下文下的单独项目符号中定义。 尽管如此，它们在应用方面还是有限的。

这意味着服务器渲染和客户端渲染可以通过在称为Universal Rendering或简称为SSR中的再水化结合起来。 人们可能会将性能缺陷视为对 Time To Interactive 的负面影响，因为 JS 由于在执行客户端 JS 并附加了事件处理程序之前对输入没有响应，因此延迟了交互性。

水合作用通过性能指标与用户体验相关。 不仅是一种技术，而且是一种旨在向用户提供预期交互体验的策略。

结论
一个原因归结为用户体验，因为最终让用户在假装和现实之间感到困惑是多么容易。

很少或没有交互性的体验，服务器渲染可以代表这些问题的更具可扩展性的解决方案。 使用 CSR 构建的依赖于大型 JavaScript 包的体验应该考虑积极的代码拆分，并确保延迟加载 JavaScript——“只在你需要的时候提供你需要的东西”。 参考

[1] Rendering on the Web (Jason Miller, Addy Osmani) in web.dev - 2019 [1] web.dev 中的 Web 渲染（Jason Miller，Addy Osmani） - 2019
[2] Hydration in web development - Wikipedia [2]网络开发中的水合作用 - 维基百科
[3] What does it mean to hydrate an object? [3]水合物体是什么意思？
[*] Cover image taken from https://www.ironin.it/ [*] 封面图片取自https://www.ironin.it/



原文:

Hydration is a term used in computer programming when generally talking through the process of filling an object with data.

An object is partially hydrated when only some of the fields are loaded into it. One may want not to load data that is not going to be used to avoid wasting bandwidth, security pitfalls, CPU cycles loading, transferring, and setting routines. [3]

When it comes to web development it usually means to fasten a loaded markup with functionality, using client-side JavaScript to add application state and interactivity to server-rendered HTML.

Hydration (or rehydration) is a technique in which client-side JavaScript converts a static HTML web page, delivered either through static hosting or server-side rendering, into a dynamic web page by attaching event handlers to the HTML elements. [2]

Rehydration: “booting up” JavaScript views on the client such that they reuse the server-rendered HTML’s DOM tree and data. [1]

Differences between rendering approaches on the web has implication in performance metrics. One must consider the trade-offs through the lens of experience.

Server rendering is a way to avoid additional round-trips for data fetching and templating on the client. The full HTML for a page is generated on the server in response to navigation. Server rendering produces: [1]

  • Fast First Paint (FP)
  • Fast First Contentful Paint (FCP)
  • Fast Time to Interactive (TTI)

This is possible because you’re just sending text and links to the user’s browser and so, it works well for a large spectrum of device and network conditions.

It sounds interesting, right? A faster TTI means a fully interactive page available to users asap. However, as with a lot of other things in development, we must consider the trade-off.

The primary drawback to this approach: generating pages on the server takes time, which can often result in a slower Time to First Byte (TTFB). [1]

Client-side rendering relies on the browser to fully render pages using JavaScript. All logic, data fetching, templating, and routing are handled on the client rather than the server. [1]

In terminology definitions presented in [1] Server-Side Rendering, Client-Side Rendering, and Rehydration are defined in separated bullets under the Rendering context. Still, they are bounded when it comes to application.

It means that server rendering and client-side rendering can be combined through rehydration in something called Universal Rendering or simply SSR. [1]

One may consider performance drawbacks as the negative impact on Time To Interactive delaying interactivity due to JS due to no response to input until the client-side JS is executed and event handlers have been attached.

Hydratation is related to the user experience through performance metrics. Not only a technique but also a strategy designed to deliver the expected interactivity experience to the user.

An infographic is available in [1] showing the server-client spectrum:

Conclusion
In 2019, developers were encouraged by Jason Miller and Addy Osmani, to consider server rendering or static rendering over a full rehydration approach. One reason comes down to User Experience due to how extremely easy is to end up leaving users confused between pretense and reality.

Experiences with little or no interactivity, server rendering can represent a more scalable solution to these issues. Experiences built with CSR that rely on large JavaScript bundles should consider aggressive code-splitting, and be sure to lazy-load JavaScript - "serve only what you need, when you need it". [1]

The "Hydration" term was removed from Angular docs in 2017.

References

[1] Rendering on the Web (Jason Miller, Addy Osmani) in web.dev - 2019
[2] Hydration in web development - Wikipedia
[3] What does it mean to hydrate an object?
[*] Cover image taken from https://www.ironin.it/
item.author: angellicaaraujo

item.title: 学校应用 | School app
item.description:
社交媒体（尤其是 Facebook 和 Medium）上谈论学校网站的人数每月继续增长。 我想知道您对学校网站的看法？

通知会显示出来，家长和学生可以通过学校的移动应用程序访问重要信息，例如财务、出勤和时间表。 大多数学校应用程序不断向家长提供关于孩子进步的持续更新的实时数字报告，这在许多方面为家长提供了便利。 数字父母晚间预订系统听起来肯定比依赖儿童安全送回家的古老纸质单据要顺畅得多。 父母和老师都是忙碌的人，因此只需选择他们喜欢的父母晚上的时段而不需要将其写在日历上，对于拥有学校通讯应用程序来说是一个非常大的积极因素。 过分关注诸如“我不能做什么”或“我有什么问题”这样的想法会造成对自己的负面信念系统，并导致我们误解我们是谁或我们可以成为什么样的人。 与老师会面是为初中和高中的家长、教职员工设计并成功实施的，为家长提供了在学年开始时与孩子的老师和其他主要学校人员进行非正式会面的机会。 创建家长可以通过您的学校应用程序直接填写和签名的数字表格。 不再需要打印和分发，不再丢失表格，不再需要解释凌乱的笔迹，也不再需要手动输入数据。

学校的应用程序配置文件是完全可定制的。 这意味着您的学校可以发布应用程序的重要更新，并链接到经常使用的服务，如食堂支付、制服商店或学习管理平台。 因此，该应用程序充当您学校生活的主屏幕，这意味着您将始终拥有触手可及的最新信息。 家长、教师、学生和管理人员不仅可以随时了解任何学校停课或紧急情况，还可以通过学校的移动应用程序接收有关下一步做什么的指示。 一个机构的教育管理系统有时由一个人处理。 虽然这对小型学校来说可能就足够了，但随着学校的发展，它可能会导致一些需要更专业管理的问题。 学校管理软件有助于减轻管理员的负担，尤其是在财务和会计应用程序方面。 通过您学校的移动应用程序让今天的学生了解最新情况。 将家长晚会系统整合到一个简单易用的平台中的学校可以减轻他们的行政负担。

创新与稳定合二为一

社交媒体帐户现在很常见，并密切关注网站以研究公司、组织，或者就您而言，研究学校。 积极使用 Twitter 的副产品将有助于宣传您的学校并展示您所做的出色工作！ 大量证据表明，家长参与有利于学生，包括提高他们的学业成绩。 当父母参与时，孩子还有其他好处——即增加学习动力、改善行为、更经常出勤，以及对家庭作业和学校的整体态度更积极。 学校应用程序可以拉近家长和学校的距离，打开与家庭的沟通渠道，并提供购买用品、支付旅行费用、预订俱乐部的空间，并单击按钮表示同意。 现有的父母可以使用学校的教职员工页面来识别他们孩子的老师（特别是如果他们的孩子忘记了他们的名字！），熟悉在父母之夜之前他们将见到的人，如果他们有任何问题，可以轻松找到联系信息或查询。 今天，学校可以通过在线学校支付将他们所有的交流集中到一个地方。

学校移动应用程序通常适用于特定机构，使用其徽标和图像，并结合其课程和计划。 它可以用来提醒家长与天气有关的学校停课，通知学生重要的公告或即将发生的事件，在学生体内建立社区，甚至提供课后活动。 学校应用程序使学校可以轻松地与家长沟通。 无论是分享出勤信息、家庭作业还是学校报告，家长都可以随时掌握孩子的进步情况。 清晰的仪表板让家长可以了解孩子的学校生活，从而在家长之夜进行更有意义的对话。 使用您的学校应用程序支付活动和学费并提供同意。 上学日的结构与小学完全不同，在小学中，学生通常与同一位老师待一整年。 当中学教师每天在多个班级有多个学生时，他们更难建立支持性关系。 学校管理解决方案中的动态报告、图表和跟踪工具可让您实时了解学校发生的一切。 您可以快速分析个别学生、小组、班级或年级组的进度，以帮助您加强变革和改进策略，缩小成绩差距。 使用“学校网站”的学校可以在内部和家长之间实现无纸化。

提高信息的可访问性

为人父母需要承担许多责任，想要充分发挥自己的作用的父母必须与孩子在家庭之外所经历的环境密切相关：学校。 教育者可能需要培训和专业发展来扩大他们的数据设施。 让教育工作者与其他教育工作者一起学习或从其他教育工作者那里学习可能特别有益。 您不会在会计师事务所工作并手动完成资产负债表。 您也不会在保险公司工作并手写保单文件。那么，为什么我们要这样评估学习者？ 今天，学生热衷于学习和评估，以匹配他们在现实工作和生活中所做的事情。 结构可以是对音乐的热爱，对诗歌的天赋，可以让所有偏好成为对修理汽车的兴趣的活动。 这是过时的东西——通过听、看和做来学习。 一所学校的网站在一个简单易用的地方为家长和家庭提供了触手可及的所有信息。 它将使您的内容保持新鲜，并减少给学校的电话和电子邮件数量。 学校可以通过学校中的家长应用程序等服务来减轻管理负担。

脚本化课程的效果是让教师成为内容和技能过程的简单传递者，而不是那些复杂地综合内容、技能和概念以创建旨在满足特定学生需求的复杂课程的教师。 学校的移动应用程序确保学习不会停止并加快进度。 学校应利用专业发展培训来帮助教师了解有效的数据共享实践。 当学校让家长参与时，他们是在以他们的机构自身利益和愿望来领导——学校工作人员是在用他们的嘴来领导。 当学校让家长参与时，他们是在以家长的自身利益（他们的愿望和梦想）为导向，努力发展真正的伙伴关系。 在这种情况下，学校工作人员正在用耳朵引导。 今天的父母在路上。 但今天的父母也在旅途中保持联系。 他们是使用智能手机上的应用程序访问信息的移动一代的一部分。 此信息还包括他们孩子的学校相关事件。 使用Homework App等产品的学校比其他学校更有优势。

提高父母的参与度

在您的网站上四处走动并找到他们需要的东西的能力反映了您学校的组织情况。 出勤是任何学生成功学习之旅的重要组成部分。 当您使用学校管理系统时，输入这些信息再简单不过了。 它组织数据，以便轻松查看学生每天和整个学期的出勤情况。 教师可以区分无故缺勤、迟到或无合理理由缺勤。 使用学校移动应用程序，您将能够立即进行交流，家长可以随时随地访问他们可能需要的一切，例如表格、信件、活动和学期日期。 技术深刻地改变了教育并扩大了教育范围。 通过书籍、音频、图像、视频等获得的大量信息触手可及，正式学习的机会也很多。 由于技术，获得学习机会是前所未有的。 学校应用程序可以成为管理学校中发生的整个交流活动并促进教师、家长和学生之间交流的工具。 学校现在可以将适用于学校的应用程序和所有其他系统整合到一个应用程序中。

学校应用程序的主要作用是充当促进所有类型学校交流的首选渠道，无论是学校更新、公告、通知、家长会还是任何其他类型的交流。 通过有效的沟通，教育工作者可以与家长建立协作关系，家长将继续在课堂之外为学生提供支持和指导。 由于敬业的父母使教育成为家庭中的一个已知价值，教师也可能会看到更高的出勤率、作业完成率和更高的分数。 由于传统教学方法（即教科书、私人导师、课程......）产生的大量费用，这些平台可以为家庭节省大量成本。 您可以在此Wikipedia页面中获取有关学校网站的更多信息。

相关文章：

彻底改变学校的沟通方式

学校应用

有效的家长晚会学校系统



原文:
